":"open","locked":false,"title":"Scoped values","user":{"login":"vchuravy","id":145258,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE0NTI1OA==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/145258?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/vchuravy","html_url":"https://github.com/vchuravy","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vchuravy/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vchuravy/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vchuravy/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vchuravy/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vchuravy/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vchuravy/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vchuravy/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vchuravy/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vchuravy/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Alternative proposal to #35833, based on a discussion with @gbaraldi.

ScopedVariables are containers whose observed value depends the current
dynamic scope. This implementation is inspired by https://openjdk.org/jeps/446

A scope is introduced with the `scoped` function that takes a lambda to
execute within the new scope. The value of a `ScopedValue` is
constant within that scope and can only be set upon introduction
of a new scope.

Scopes are propagated across tasks boundaries.

In contrast to #35833 the storage of the per-scope data does not require copies 
upon scope entry. This also means that libraries can use scoped variables without
paying for scoped variables introduces in other libraries.

Finding the current value of a `ScopedValue`, involves walking the
scope chain upwards and checking if the scoped variable has a value
for the current or one of its parent scopes. This means the cost of
a lookup scales with the depth of the dynamic scoping. This is amortized
by perform some caching.

https://github.com/vchuravy/ScopedValues.jl provides an implementation
reusing the logstate trick from `ContextVariablesX.jl`. We could use it
as an implementation from Julia 1.7+ on-wards.

to be fixed in v1.10

v1.8
```julia
julia> VERSION
v"1.8.4"

julia> v = collect(Any, 1:1000);

julia> i = 1;

julia> using BenchmarkTools

julia> @btime vcat($v, $i);
  515.272 ns (1 allocation: 8.00 KiB)

julia> @which vcat(v, i)
vcat(A::Union{Number, LinearAlgebra.AbstractTriangular{T, A} where {T, A<:(Matrix)}, LinearAlgebra.Adjoint{<:Any, <:Vector}, LinearAlgebra.Bidiagonal, LinearAlgebra.Diagonal, LinearAlgebra.Hermitian{T, A} where {T, A<:(Matrix)}, LinearAlgebra.SymTridiagonal, LinearAlgebra.Symmetric{T, A} where {T, A<:(Matrix)}, LinearAlgebra.Transpose{<:Any, <:Vector}, LinearAlgebra.Tridiagonal, Matrix, Vector}...) in LinearAlgebra at /Applications/Julia-1.8.app/Contents/Resources/julia/share/julia/stdlib/v1.8/LinearAlgebra/src/special.jl:419
```

v1.9
```julia
julia> VERSION
v"1.9.2"

julia> v = collect(Any, 1:1000);

julia> i = 1;

julia> using BenchmarkTools

julia> @btime vcat($v, $i);
  1.050 μs (13 allocations: 8.28 KiB)

julia> @which vcat(v, i)
vcat(A::Union{Number, Matrix, Vector}...)
     @ Base array.jl:1957
```

v1.10
```julia
julia> VERSION
v"1.10.0-beta1"

julia> v = collect(Any, 1:1000);

julia> i = 1;

julia> using BenchmarkTools

julia> @btime vcat($v, $i);
  521.382 ns (1 allocation: 8.00 KiB)

julia> @which vcat(v, i)
vcat(A::Union{Number, AbstractArray}...)
     @ Base abstractarray.jl:1990

was hoping to replace a misuse of `Threads.Condition` with a `Base.Event`, but discovered that you can't pass a value via an `Event` -- is there a reason for that?

The code looked roughly like
```julia
c = Threads.Condition()
while !done
    future = remotecall(f, worker)
    @spawn begin
        try
            val = fetch($fut)
            notify(c, val)
        catch e
            notify(c, e; error=true)
        end
    end
    try 
        result = wait(c)
    catch 
        ...
    end
end
```
(actual code: https://github.com/JuliaTesting/ReTestItems.jl/blob/v1.7.1/src/ReTestItems.jl#L388-L413)

And the issue here is we could `notify` before we `wait`, so i was going to replace the use of `Threads.Condition()` with `Base.Event(true)`, so that the `wait` would not block if `notify` had already been called, but discovered that just replacing one with the other is not possible because there is no `notify(::Event, val; error::Bool=false)` method","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50183/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50183/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/pulls/comments/1066088938","pull_request_review_id":1242698596,"id":1066088938,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOBOhtSc4_iznq","diff_hunk":"@@ -0,0 +1,43 @@
+module ProjectManifestTest
+
+import ..Pkg # ensure we are using the correct Pkg
+using Test, Pkg
+using ..Utils
+
+temp_pkg_dir() do project_path
+    @testset "test Project.toml manifest" begin
+        mktempdir() do dir
+            path = abspath(joinpath(dirname(pathof(Pkg)), "../test", "test_packages", "monorepo"))
+            cp(path, joinpath(dir, "monorepo"))
+            cd(joinpath(dir, "monorepo")) do
+                with_current_env() do
+                    Pkg.develop(path="packages/B")
+                end
+            end
+            # test subpackage instantiates/tests
+            # the order of operations here is important
+            # when we instantiate and dev a dependency to the C subpackage

recently discovered that loading in Base supports specifying an alternative `Manifest.toml` in a `Project.toml`. While playing around with this though, I discovered that Pkg.jl doesn't play as nice with this. It supports the parsing/storing of it, but gets confused often because so many path calculations are made relative to the _project file_ instead of the now-different manifest file.

This is relevant when there are devv-ed packages in the specified Manifest and the relative path calculations should be made wrt to the _manifest_ not the _project_.

The changes here, hence, are mainly about auditing uses of `env.project_file` and changing them to `env.manifest_file` if necessary. The cases where `manifest_file` should be used, as far as I understand, are where the `PackageSpec` we're computing a path for came from a manifest entry instead of a project dep or elsewhere.

The other change in this PR is in `prune_manifest` where we actually _don't_ want to prune the manifest if the project doesn't "own" it. I'm calculating that by checking `dirname(env.manifest_file) != dirname(env.project_file)`, but we could also potentially just check `env.pkg.manifest` if it's populated, but it wasn't clear if that would be a more sure thing since we're trying to query if the Manifest isn't the recently discovered that loading in Base supports specifying an alternative `Manifest.toml` in a `Project.toml`. While playing around with this though, I discovered that Pkg.jl doesn't play as nice with this. It supports the parsing/storing of it, but gets confused often because so many path calculations are made relative to the _project file_ instead of the now-different manifest file.

This is relevant when there are devv-ed packages in the specified Manifest and the relative path calculations should be made wrt to the _manifest_ not the _project_.

The changes here, hence, are mainly about auditing uses of `env.project_file` and changing them to `env.manifest_file` if necessary. The cases where `manifest_file` should be used, as far as I understand, are where the `PackageSpec` we're computing a path for came from a manifest entry instead of a project dep or elsewhere.

The other change in this PR is in `prune_manifest` where we actually _don't_ want to prune the manifest if the project doesn't "own" it. I'm calculating that by checking `dirname(env.manifest_file) != dirname(env.project_file)`, but we could also potentially just check `env.pkg.manifest` if it's populated, but it wasn't clear if that would be a more sure thing since we're trying to query if the Manifest isn't the recently discovered that loading in Base supports specifying an alternative `Manifest.toml` in a `Project.toml`. While playing around with this though, I discovered that Pkg.jl doesn't play as nice with this. It supports the parsing/storing of it, but gets confused often because so many path calculations are made relative to the _project file_ instead of the now-different manifest file.

This is relevant when there are devv-ed packages in the specified Manifest and the relative path calculations should be made wrt to the _manifest_ not the _project_.

The changes here, hence, are mainly about auditing uses of `env.project_file` and changing them to `env.manifest_file` if necessary. The cases where `manifest_file` should be used, as far as I understand, are where the `PackageSpec` we're computing a path for came from a manifest entry instead of a project dep or elsewhere.

The other change in this PR is in `prune_manifest` where we actually _don't_ want to prune the manifest if the project doesn't "own" it. I'm calculating that by checking `dirname(env.manifest_file) != dirname(env.project_file)`, but we could also potentially just check `env.pkg.manifest` if it's populated, but it wasn't clear if that would be a more sure thing since we're trying to query if the Manifest isn't the when profiling allocation in HTTP requests (via HTTP.jl) that `verify_hosts` was allocating more than necessary: `uppercase(transport)` allocates a new string. Instead we can just check both upper and lower case manually. For code like this that might be called a lot, i thought this small improvement might still be worth it.

before
```jl
julia> url = "http://httpbin.org/anything";

julia> @btime verify_host($url, t) setup=(t=rand(["tls", "ssh", "foo"]))
  1.058 μs (7 allocations: 344 bytes)
```

now
```jl
julia> @btime verify_host($url, t) setup=(t=rand(["tls", "ssh", "foo"]))
  993.538 ns (4 allocations: 224 bytes)
true
```
The remaining allocations come from the successful `match(re, url)` calls in allocations","user":{"login":"nickrobinson251","id":13448787,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEzNDQ4Nzg3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/13448787?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251","html_url":"https://github.com/nickrobinson251","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Adds an ~`@allocs`~ `@allocations` macro (name TBD) which behaves similarly to `@allocated` except for returning a count of the allocations during evaluation of the expression rather than the total size in bytes of the memory allocated.

This uses `Base.gc_alloc_count` so returns a single number combining all types of allocations (malloc, realloc, poolalloc, bigalloc), like what is printed via `@time`. The more granular break down is still available from `@timed` (or printed via `@timev`). i think the single number is useful for some cases (when you're happy to treat all allocations as equal).

The motivation is to make it just as easy to get number of allocations as it is to get number of bytes allocated. For example, at RAI we have some people investigating sources of allocations and are often more concerned about the number of allocations than the size of memory allocated, yet i still see people reach for `@allocated` to get a single-number sumarry, because it's just a much easier and more discoverable interface than something like `Base.gc_alloc_count((@timed foo()).gcstats)`.

Opened this as a PR as it wasn't too much harder than opening an issue - discussion welcome! Would appreciate pointers on implementation and testing too, if we're happy with this in prospect. Thanks!

For example, at RAI we have some people investigating sources of allocations and are often more concerned about the number of allocations than the size of memory allocated, yet i still see people reach for `@allocated` to get a single-number sumarry, because it's just a much easier and more discoverable interface than something like `Base.gc_alloc_count((@timed foo()).gcstats)`.

Opened this as a PR as it wasn't too much harder than opening an issue - discussion welcome! Would appreciate pointers on implementation and testing too, if we're happy with this in prospect. Thanks!

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/comments/1012256410","pull_request_review_id":1165952682,"id":1012256410,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOABkWpM48Vc6a","diff_hunk":"@@ -98,6 +98,51 @@ Base.EnvDict with "N" entries:
 """
 const ENV = EnvDict()
 
+"""
+    Base.get_bool_env(name::String, default::Bool = false)::Bool
+
+Evaluate whether the value of environnment variable `name` is truthy or falsy,
+and error if none are recognized. If the variable is not set, or is set to "",
+return `default`.
+
+Recognized values are:
+    truthy: "t", "true", "y", "yes", "1"
+    falsy:  "f", "false", "n", "no", "0"
+"""
+function get_bool_env(name::String, default::Bool = false)
+    haskey(ENV, name) || return default
+    val = lowercase(get(ENV, name, ""))
+    if isempty(val)
+        return default
+    elseif is_truthy(val)
+        return true
+    elseif is_falsy(val)
+        return false
+    else
+        error("""
+        Failed to parse the value `$val` of environment variable `$name` as Boolean.
+        Recognized values are:
+            truthy: "t", "true", "y", "yes", "1"
+            falsy:  "f", "false", "n", "no", "0"
+        """)
+    end
+end

+    Base.is_truthy(str::String)
+
+Evaluate whether a string is "truthy" i.e. representative of a `true`.

 """
 const ENV = EnvDict()
 
+"""
+    Base.get_bool_env(name::String, default::Bool = false)::Bool
+
+Evaluate whether the value of environnment variable `name` is truthy or falsy,
+and error if none are recognized. If the variable is not set, or is set to "",
+return `default`.
+
+Recognized values are:
+    truthy: "t", "true", "y", "yes", "1"
+    falsy:  "f", "false", "n", "no", "0"
+"""
+function get_bool_env(name::String, default::Bool = false)
+    haskey(ENV, name) || return default
+    val = lowercase(get(ENV, name, ""))","path":"base/env.jl","position":17,"original_position":17,"commit_id":"6909666574c296905f38a08a14ce8eb3d1eb19eb","original_commit_id":"6909666574c296905f38a08a14ce8eb3d1eb19eb","user":{"login":"nickrobinson251","id":13448787,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEzNDQ4Nzg3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/13448787?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251","html_url":"https://github.com/nickrobinson251","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"probably this doesn't matter, as this won't be called in a performance critical place, but... maybe we should avoid this allocating when possible, by checking if the string is already lowercase (which i think should be okay overhead in practice since we're not expecting really long strings here)

+    Base.get_bool_env(name::String, default::Bool = false)::Bool
+
+Evaluate whether the value of environnment variable `name` is truthy or falsy,

+return `default`.
+
+Recognized values are:
+    truthy: "t", "true", "y", "yes", "1"
+    falsy:  "f", "false", "n", "no", "0"
+"""
+function get_bool_env(name::String, default::Bool = false)
+    haskey(ENV, name) || return default

`@allocations` macro for getting number of allocations","user":{"login":"nickrobinson251","id":13448787,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEzNDQ4Nzg3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/13448787?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251","html_url":"https://github.com/nickrobinson251","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2022-10-28T14:58:22Z","updated_at":"2022-11-03T14:07:37Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/47367","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/47367","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/47367.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/47367.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"Adds an ~`@allocs`~ `@allocations` macro (name TBD) which behaves similarly to `@allocated` except for returning a count of the allocations during evaluation of the expression rather than the total size in bytes of the memory allocated.

This uses `Base.gc_alloc_count` so returns a single number combining all types of allocations (malloc, realloc, poolalloc, bigalloc), like what is printed via `@time`. The more granular break down is still available from `@timed` (or printed via `@timev`). i think the single number is useful for some cases (when you're happy to treat all allocations as equal).

The motivation is to make it just as easy to get number of allocations as it is to get number of bytes allocated. For example, at RAI we have some people investigating sources of allocations and are often more concerned about the number of allocations than the size of memory allocated, yet i still see people reach for `@allocated` to get a single-number sumarry, because it's just a much easier and more discoverable interface than something like `Base.gc_alloc_count((@timed foo()).gcstats)`.

Opened this as a PR as it wasn't too much harder than opening an issue - discussion welcome! Would appreciate pointers on implementation and testing too, if we're happy with this in prospect. Thanks!

request","color":"88c429","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":54,"created_at":"2018-07-10T20:58:28Z","updated_at":"2022-11-23T13:03:51Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Currently `Project.toml` is not enough to use a package, unless all it's deps can be found and installed from registries. I think it would be nice to include some information in `Project.toml` about where to find the deps. In particular two usecases come to mind:

1. Projects which depend on unregistred packages could put a url to the package
```
name = "Foo"
uuid = "4bdd1c6b-68bb-4dfe-a423-fa0d59eb7384"
version = "0.1.0"

[deps]
Bar = "ce02910b-3083-40c5-958c-8fd1036218f1"

[where]
Bar = "https://www.bar.com"
```
2. Projects with deps in the same repo can put the (relative) path
```
name = "Foo"
uuid = "4bdd1c6b-68bb-4dfe-a423-fa0d59eb7384"
version = "0.1.0"

[deps]
Bar = "ce02910b-3083-40c5-958c-8fd1036218f1"

[where]
Bar = "modules/Bar"
```

This would make `Project.toml` a bit more standalone; I can give that to someone and things will work, but if I want that someone to obtain the exact state I have, then I can also send `Manifest.toml`.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/492/reactions","total_count":74,"+1":55,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":19,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/492/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/comments/1325025341","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/492#issuecomment-1325025341","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/492","id":1325025341,"node_id":"IC_kwDOBOhtSc5O-kg9","user":{"login":"nickrobinson251","id":13448787,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEzNDQ4Nzg3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/13448787?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251","html_url":"https://github.com/nickrobinson251","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-11-23T13:03:50Z","updated_at":"2022-11-23T13:03:50Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"> should I allow `[compat]` sections in subpackages

I think so, yes... though perhaps we want to automatically throw a `ERROR: Unsatisfiable requirements` if the SubPkg `compat` conflicts with the ParentPkg `compat` when instantiating the SubPkg enviroment? (I've no idea how easy this is to implement in Pkg.jl; but i hope it's fairly easy)

I think there are a couple benefits to allowing `[compat]` sections in subpackages:
1. it keeps the Project.toml files of subpackages similar to other Project.toml files; there's a `parent =` entry but no other differences, as opposed to `parent =` implies no `compat` 
2. it provides for a more local information e.g. you can see that it's SubPkg1 that requires Parsers v1.2, and SubPkg2 requires Parsers v1.3, rather than just seeing v1.3 in ParentPkg.

Bit of thinking out loud:

Something maybe helpful to reiterate (i guess it's assumed by this whole issue, but i keep having to remind myself): we're assuming that a SubPkg lives at HEAD i.e. that ParentPkg will always use the latest revision of SubPkg, i.e. we don't use the `version` entry in `SubPkg/Project.toml` and the `ParentPkg/Project.toml` doesn't set a `compat` entry for SubPkg (since SubPkg isn't in a registry we wouldn't know how to look up earlier versions of SubPkg; we'd be using a registry if we wanted that kind of functionality).

And then a natural thought is "How do we make sure that the SubPkg dependencies are always compatible with the ParentPkg?" (since we need them to be always be in sync / updated together when necessary). And one possible conclusion is "we make sure they cannot have incompatible dependencies by keeping all dependency compat info in the `ParentPkg/Project.toml`".

But if we'd have to look at `ParentPkg/Project.toml` when instantiating SubPkg environment anyway, then i don't see why we can't also have `compat` entries in `SubPkg/Project.toml` and just hit an `ERROR: Unsatisfiable requirements` if the two entries can't both be satisfied.

For example, in a repo like this:
```jl
ParentPkg/
├── Project.toml
├── Manifest.toml
├── src/
├── test/
└── packages/
    ├── SubPkg1
    │   ├── Project.toml
    │   └── src/
    │   └── test/
    └── SubPkg2
        ├── Project.toml
        ├── src/
        └── test/
```

If `ParentPkg/Project.toml` looks like
```toml
name = "ParentPkg"
uuid = "42de0a69-1ddd-5017-9359-2bf0b02dc9f0"
version = "1.0.0"

[deps]
SubPkg1 = "52de0a69-1ddd-5017-9359-2bf0b02dc9f0"
Parsers = "69de0a69-1ddd-5017-9359-2bf0b02dc9f0"

[compat]
Parsers = "1"
```
and `SubPkg1/Project.toml` looks like
```toml 
name = "SubPkg1"
uuid = "52de0a69-1ddd-5017-9359-2bf0b02dc9f0"

[deps]
Parsers = "69de0a69-1ddd-5017-9359-2bf0b02dc9f0"

[compat]
Parsers = "2"
```
then when updating the ParentPkg environment we will get
```julia
(ParentPkg) pkg> up
ERROR: Unsatisfiable requirements detected for package Parsers [69de0a69]:
 Parsers [69de0a69] log:
 ├─possible versions are: 0.1.0-2.5.1 or uninstalled
 ├─restricted to versions 2 by SubPkg1 [52de0a69], leaving only versions 2.0.0-2.5.1
 │ └─SubPkg1 [52de0a69] log:
 │   ├─possible versions are: 0.0.0 or uninstalled
 │   └─SubPkg1 [52de0a69] is fixed to version 0.0.0
 └─restricted to versions 1 by ParentPkg [42de0a69] — no versions left
   └─ParentPkg [42de0a69] log:
     ├─possible versions are: 1.0.0 or uninstalled
     └─ParentPkg [42de0a69] is fixed to version 1.0.0
```

But the thing i think we want is to hit this error when working in the SubPkg environment. Currently we'd get
```julia
(SubPkg1) pkg> up
    Updating registry at `~/.julia/registries/General.toml`
    Updating `~/projects/ParentPkg/packages/SubPkg1/Project.toml`
  [69de0a69] + Parsers v2.5.1
```
i.e. currently we install Parsers v2 when actually Parsers v2 is incompatible with ParentPkg, but we want to hit an error like we do in the ParentPkg environment.

As i say, I've no idea how easy it'd be to actually implement this in Pkg.jl

All that said... is the status quo even that bad? i.e. we allow `compat` in SubPkg1 and if it's in conflict with the `compat` in ParentPkg we see that error when in the ParentPkg environment.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/comments/1325025341/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Seems the simplest way to split up the options and close #40228 ...I'd also be happy to remove the one for disabling module color, since that's not (yet) been requested. - (but we could have e.g. three: 1 for modules + 1 for the arguments + 1 for the location). - should these be binary true/false or allow custom colours? - i've gone for binary true/false. Again, seems simplest. Also matches the existing `JULIA_STACKTRACE_*` variables. - by default (`true`) you get the color printing that Base sets - by setting `false` you can disable the color printing, recovering something very similar to the printing for Julia v1.0 - v1.5, - If we wanted to allow specifying colours we'd have to figure out how, and i don't have a use-case for that, and it seems more like the role of an external package anyway. > 
> For instance, it's possible to do
> ```@time_imports include("foo.jl")```


It seems confusing (to me) for this to show a "total" that's _not_ the sum of the individual import times. In fact it's just timing a different thing. Personally I think it'd be better to just recommend `@time @time_imports ...` for this so it's explicit what's going on -- perhaps this idiom can be noted prominently in the docs related to `@time_imports`. As is, I expect this to result in a lot of questions like "What causes the total to be so much bigger??". 

In contrast, I think showing the sum _would_ be a useful addition -- i know i've wished for that a few times! -- and _maybe_ was the intended request of #46850 (idk, @baggepinnen would have to clarify 😄)

function","user":{"login":"jakobnissen","id":23193950,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIzMTkzOTUw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/23193950?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen","html_url":"https://github.com/jakobnissen","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":269951426,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwyNjk5NTE0MjY=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/strings","name":"strings","color":"006b75","default":false,"description":""Strings!""}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":14,"created_at":"2021-12-26T18:27:55Z","updated_at":"2022-10-10T15:31:05Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/43557","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/43557","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/43557.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/43557.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"Often, when processing text, you run into the need to split some string at a delimiter. Julia already provides the `eachsplit` iterator, as well as the `split` function. However, the `split` function returns an array and hence forces an allocation, which makes it unsuitable in performance sensitive code. Working directly with a `eachsplit` iterator is possible in v1.8, but it's bothersome to call `iterate(itr, state)` multiple times to split a line in say, 5 parts.

This issue is not unique to Julia. Python provides the method `str.partition`, which splits a string exactly once and returns the result as a tuple. Rust provides the similar `str::split_once`. Convenience methods like this are _very_ useful when text processing. In Julia, however, we can generalize it to N splits, while still avoiding allocations.

This PR adds the `split_n` function. I believe this is worth adding another export to Base because
* Splitting is a common task with strings
* The API provided by `split_n` is both convenient and can be implemented fast enough that users won't have to roll their own
* It is not completely trivial to write this function, and especially to optimize it

The provided implementation is not optimized, because it still allocates. Such optimizations could be added later if the API is acceptable.

### Things to consider
* Should we have this in Base?
* Name bikeshedding: Should it be called something else like `splitn` or `splittuple`?
* Is it _actually_ possible to optimize this to be allocation free and hence substantially faster than `split`? If not, this function is pointless","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/43557/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/43557/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1273489836","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/43557#issuecomment-1273489836","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/43557","id":1273489836,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5L5-ms","user":{"login":"nickrobinson251","id":13448787,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEzNDQ4Nzg3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/13448787?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251","html_url":"https://github.com/nickrobinson251","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-10-10T15:31:05Z","updated_at":"2022-10-10T15:31:05Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Can we bikeshed the name a little? `split_n` just looks a little odd to me. I don't think it fits any existing naming convention in Base.
- Perhaps just because Base omits the underscore in most cases, so perhaps `splitn`? 
- Alternatively we have `n` as the prefix in `ntuple`, `ndims` -- which suggest `nsplit` or `nsplits`?
- If we want something perhaps more informative than "n" how about "into" like `split_into(str, 3)` which i think reads okay (`split_into(str, 3)` reads as "str split into 3") or (split_into(str, 3, 'a') reads as "str split into 3 on 'a'") -- so `split_into`?

I don't really love any spelling here, but `split_n` still looks most odd.

Final bikeshed-y thought: Do we have any other functions exported from Base for which `Val` _must_ be passed? (Maybe `reshape`?) I wonder if there should be a method that accepts an Int and wraps it in `Val`? {"action":"opened","number":47367,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/47367","id":1103216760,"node_id":"PR_kwDOABkWpM5BwcB4","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/47367","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/47367.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/47367.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47367","number":47367,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"Add `@allocs` macro for getting number of allocations","user":{"login":"nickrobinson251","id":13448787,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEzNDQ4Nzg3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/13448787?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251","html_url":"https://github.com/nickrobinson251","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Adds an `@allocs` macro (name TBD) which behaves similarly to `@allocated` except for returning a count of the allocations during evaluation of the expression rather than the total size in bytes of the memory allocated.

This uses `Base.gc_alloc_count` so returns a single number combining all types of allocations (malloc, realloc, poolalloc, bigalloc), like what is printed via `@time`. The more granular break down is still available from `@timed` (or printed via `@timev`). i think the single number is useful for some cases (when you're happy to treat all allocations as equal).

The motivation is to make it just as easy to get number of allocations as it is to get number of bytes allocated. For example, at RAI we have some people investigating sources of allocations and are often more concerned about the number of allocations than the size of memory allocated, yet i still see people reach for `@allocated` to get a single-number sumarry, because it's just a much easier and more discoverable interface than something like `Base.gc_alloc_count((@timed foo()).gcstats).

Open this as a PR as it wasn't too much harder than opening an issue - discussion welcome! Would appreciate pointers on implementation and testing too, if we're happy with this in prospect. Thanks!

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/comments/1008163965","pull_request_review_id":1160211933,"id":1008163965,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOABkWpM48F1x9","diff_hunk":"@@ -430,6 +430,33 @@ macro allocated(ex)
     end
 end
 
+"""
+    @allocs
+
+A macro to evaluate an expression, discard the resulting value, and instead return the
+total number of allocations during evaluation of the expression.
+
+See also [`@allocated`](@ref), [`@time`](@ref), [`@timev`](@ref), [`@timed`](@ref),
+and [`@elapsed`](@ref).
+
+```julia-repl
+julia> @allocs `@allocs` macro for getting number of allocations","user":{"login":"nickrobinson251","id":13448787,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEzNDQ4Nzg3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/13448787?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251","html_url":"https://github.com/nickrobinson251","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Adds an `@allocs` macro (name TBD) which behaves similarly to `@allocated` except for returning a count of the allocations during evaluation of the expression rather than the total size in bytes of the memory allocated.

This uses `Base.gc_alloc_count` so returns a single number combining all types of allocations (malloc, realloc, poolalloc, bigalloc), like what is printed via `@time`. The more granular break down is still available from `@timed` (or printed via `@timev`). i think the single number is useful for some cases (when you're happy to treat all allocations as equal).

The motivation is to make it just as easy to get number of allocations as it is to get number of bytes allocated. For example, at RAI we have some people investigating sources of allocations and are often more concerned about the number of allocations than the size of memory allocated, yet i still see people reach for `@allocated` to get a single-number sumarry, because it's just a much easier and more discoverable interface than something like `Base.gc_alloc_count((@timed foo()).gcstats).

Open this as a PR as it wasn't too much harder than opening an issue - discussion welcome! Would appreciate pointers on implementation and testing too, if we're happy with this in prospect. Thanks!

allocations","user":{"login":"nickrobinson251","id":13448787,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEzNDQ4Nzg3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/13448787?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251","html_url":"https://github.com/nickrobinson251","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Adds an `@allocs` macro (name TBD) which behaves similarly to `@allocated` except for returning a count of the allocations during evaluation of the expression rather than the total size in bytes of the memory allocated.

This uses `Base.gc_alloc_count` so returns a single number combining all types of allocations (malloc, realloc, poolalloc, bigalloc), like what is printed via `@time`. The more granular break down is still available from `@timed` (or printed via `@timev`). i think the single number is useful for some cases (when you're happy to treat all allocations as equal).

The motivation is to make it just as easy to get number of allocations as it is to get number of bytes allocated. For example, at RAI we have some people investigating sources of allocations and are often more concerned about the number of allocations than the size of memory allocated, yet i still see people reach for `@allocated` to get a single-number sumarry, because it's just a much easier and more discoverable interface than something like `Base.gc_alloc_count((@timed foo()).gcstats).

Open this as a PR as it wasn't too much harder than opening an issue - discussion welcome! Would appreciate pointers on implementation and testing too, if we're happy with this in prospect. Thanks!

e":"open","locked":false,"title":"Add allocations","user":{"login":"nickrobinson251","id":13448787,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEzNDQ4Nzg3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/13448787?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251","html_url":"https://github.com/nickrobinson251","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Adds an `@allocs` macro (name TBD) which behaves similarly to `@allocated` except for returning a count of the allocations during evaluation of the expression rather than the total size in bytes of the memory allocated.

This uses `Base.gc_alloc_count` so returns a single number combining all types of allocations (malloc, realloc, poolalloc, bigalloc), like what is printed via `@time`. The more granular break down is still available from `@timed` (or printed via `@timev`). i think the single number is useful for some cases (when you're happy to treat all allocations as equal).

The motivation is to make it just as easy to get number of allocations as it is to get number of bytes allocated. For example, at RAI we have some people investigating sources of allocations and are often more concerned about the number of allocations than the size of memory allocated, yet i still see people reach for `@allocated` to get a single-number sumarry, because it's just a much easier and more discoverable interface than something like `Base.gc_alloc_count((@timed foo()).gcstats).

Open this as a PR as it wasn't too much harder than opening an issue - discussion welcome! Would appreciate pointers on implementation and testing too, if we're happy with this in prospect. Thanks!

e":"open","locked":false,"title":"Add `@allocs` macro for getting number of allocations","user":{"login":"nickrobinson251","id":13448787,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEzNDQ4Nzg3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/13448787?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251","html_url":"https://github.com/nickrobinson251","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Adds an `@allocs` macro (name TBD) which behaves similarly to `@allocated` except for returning a count of the allocations during evaluation of the expression rather than the total size in bytes of the memory allocated.

This uses `Base.gc_alloc_count` so returns a single number combining all types of allocations (malloc, realloc, poolalloc, bigalloc), like what is printed via `@time`. The more granular break down is still available from `@timed` (or printed via `@timev`). i think the single number is useful for some cases (when you're happy to treat all allocations as equal).

The motivation is to make it just as easy to get number of allocations as it is to get number of bytes allocated. For example, at RAI we have some people investigating sources of allocations and are often more concerned about the number of allocations than the size of memory allocated, yet i still see people reach for `@allocated` to get a single-number sumarry, because it's just a much easier and more discoverable interface than something like `Base.gc_alloc_count((@timed foo()).gcstats).

Open this as a PR as it wasn't too much harder than opening an issue - discussion welcome! Would appreciate pointers on implementation and testing too, if we're happy with this in prospect. Thanks!

`@allocations` macro for getting number of allocations","user":{"login":"nickrobinson251","id":13448787,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEzNDQ4Nzg3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/13448787?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251","html_url":"https://github.com/nickrobinson251","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Adds an ~`@allocs`~ `@allocations` macro (name TBD) which behaves similarly to `@allocated` except for returning a count of the allocations during evaluation of the expression rather than the total size in bytes of the memory allocated.

This uses `Base.gc_alloc_count` so returns a single number combining all types of allocations (malloc, realloc, poolalloc, bigalloc), like what is printed via `@time`. The more granular break down is still available from `@timed` (or printed via `@timev`). i think the single number is useful for some cases (when you're happy to treat all allocations as equal).

The motivation is to make it just as easy to get number of allocations as it is to get number of bytes allocated. For example, at RAI we have some people investigating sources of allocations and are often more concerned about the number of allocations than the size of memory allocated, yet i still see people reach for `@allocated` to get a single-number sumarry, because it's just a much easier and more discoverable interface than something like `Base.gc_alloc_count((@timed foo()).gcstats)`.

Opened this as a PR as it wasn't too much harder than opening an issue - discussion welcome! Would appreciate pointers on implementation and testing too, if we're happy with this in prospect. Thanks!

ate":"open","locked":false,"title":"Add `@allocations` macro for getting number of allocations","user":{"login":"nickrobinson251","id":13448787,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEzNDQ4Nzg3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/13448787?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251","html_url":"https://github.com/nickrobinson251","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Adds an ~`@allocs`~ `@allocations` macro (name TBD) which behaves similarly to `@allocated` except for returning a count of the allocations during evaluation of the expression rather than the total size in bytes of the memory allocated.

This uses `Base.gc_alloc_count` so returns a single number combining all types of allocations (malloc, realloc, poolalloc, bigalloc), like what is printed via `@time`. The more granular break down is still available from `@timed` (or printed via `@timev`). i think the single number is useful for some cases (when you're happy to treat all allocations as equal).

The motivation is to make it just as easy to get number of allocations as it is to get number of bytes allocated. For example, at RAI we have some people investigating sources of allocations and are often more concerned about the number of allocations than the size of memory allocated, yet i still see people reach for `@allocated` to get a single-number sumarry, because it's just a much easier and more discoverable interface than something like `Base.gc_alloc_count((@timed foo()).gcstats)`.

Opened this as a PR as it wasn't too much harder than opening an issue - discussion welcome! Would appreciate pointers on implementation and testing too, if we're happy with this in prospect. Thanks!

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46236","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46236/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46236/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46236/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46236","id":1324253701,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5O7oIF","number":46236,"title":"Always show "closest candidate" when there is a `MethodError`","user":{"login":"nickrobinson251","id":13448787,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEzNDQ4Nzg3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/13448787?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251","html_url":"https://github.com/nickrobinson251","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2022-08-01T11:18:11Z","updated_at":"2022-08-01T11:18:11Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Currently we have heuristics for building a list of "closest candidate" methods to display when a user hits a `MethodError`, but these heuristics can result in no methods making the list... so we show _none_ of the valid methods which seems less helpful than showing _some_ (/any) of the valid methods.

For example, this seems to happens if none of the argument types match and more than two arguments were given. 
```julia
julia> foo(x::Int, y::Int, z=1, a=2) = 1;

julia> foo("a")  
ERROR: MethodError: no method matching foo(::String)
Closest candidates are:
  foo(::Int64, ::Int64) at REPL[1]:1
  foo(::Int64, ::Int64, ::Any) at REPL[1]:1
  foo(::Int64, ::Int64, ::Any, ::Any) at REPL[1]:1
Stacktrace:
 [1] top-level scope
   @ REPL[2]:1

julia> foo("a", "b")  # why no "Closest candidates"?
ERROR: MethodError: no method matching foo(::String, ::String)
Stacktrace:
 [1] top-level scope
   @ REPL[3]:1
   ```
https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/35ac6e1823a2145fdbd8273d62cc19f10bde3543/base/errorshow.jl#L484

I don't know why this heuristic was chosen, and I think we should remove such heuristics that discard methods. We can still show only 3 candidates and ordered by some measure of "closeness", but showing _some_ methods seems strictly more informative than showing none.

very related to https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/32470 (but i've opened this separately as this is a more narrow request and that one seem to have become about _how to_ print this information in a good way, rather than _which_ methods to print)","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46236/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46236/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/2436","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/2436/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/2436/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/2436/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/2436","id":833682443,"node_id":"MDU6SXNzdWU4MzM2ODI0NDM=","number":2436,"title":"Progress bar glitch on narrow terminal","user":{"login":"fonsp","id":6933510,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjY5MzM1MTA=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/6933510?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp","html_url":"https://github.com/fonsp","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fonsp/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2021-03-17T11:41:38Z","updated_at":"2022-07-01T11:03:40Z","closed_at":"2021-03-18T08:31:35Z","author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"If the terminal is wide enough, the progress bar looks great:


https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/6933510/111461790-f2120280-871d-11eb-9e2a-691c4c8da786.mov


but if the terminal is **too narrow**, every progress bar update is rendered on a new line:


https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/6933510/111461866-035b0f00-871e-11eb-878d-30f375f67b51.mov

- The use-case here is when a DenseAxisArray is created with `InlineString` keys but where this is an implementation detail and we want to be able to index with regular a `String`
  
Before:
```julia
julia> using JuMP, InlineStrings

julia> daa = JuMP.Containers.DenseAxisArray(reshape(1:6, 2, :), 1:2, ["a","b","c"])
2-dimensional DenseAxisArray{Int64,2,...} with index sets:
    Dimension 1, 1:2
    Dimension 2, ["a", "b", "c"]
And data, a 2×3 Matrix{Int64}:
 1  3  5
 2  4  6

julia> daa[1, "b"]
3

julia> daa[1, InlineString("b")]
ERROR: KeyError: key "b" not found
Stacktrace:
 [1] getindex(#unused#::JuMP.Containers._AxisLookup{Dict{String, Int64}}, key::String1)
   @ JuMP.Containers ~/.julia/packages/JuMP/Y4piv/src/Containers/DenseAxisArray.jl:18
 [2] _getindex_recurse(data::Tuple{JuMP.Containers._AxisLookup{Dict{String, Int64}}}, keys::Tuple{String1}, condition::JuMP.Containers.var"#10#12")
   @ JuMP.Containers ~/.julia/packages/JuMP/Y4piv/src/Containers/DenseAxisArray.jl:309
 [3] _getindex_recurse(data::Tuple{JuMP.Containers._AxisLookup{Tuple{Int64, Int64}}, JuMP.Containers._AxisLookup{Dict{String, Int64}}}, keys::Tuple{Int64, String1}, condition::JuMP.Containers.var"#10#12")
   @ JuMP.Containers ~/.julia/packages/JuMP/Y4piv/src/Containers/DenseAxisArray.jl:308
 [4] to_index(A::JuMP.Containers.DenseAxisArray{Int64, 2, Tuple{UnitRange{Int64}, Vector{String}}, Tuple{JuMP.Containers._AxisLookup{Tuple{Int64, Int64}}, JuMP.Containers._AxisLookup{Dict{String, Int64}}}}, idx::Tuple{Int64, String1})
   @ JuMP.Containers ~/.julia/packages/JuMP/Y4piv/src/Containers/DenseAxisArray.jl:318
 [5] getindex(::JuMP.Containers.DenseAxisArray{Int64, 2, Tuple{UnitRange{Int64}, Vector{String}}, Tuple{JuMP.Containers._AxisLookup{Tuple{Int64, Int64}}, JuMP.Containers._AxisLookup{Dict{String, Int64}}}}, ::Int64, ::String1)
   @ JuMP.Containers ~/.julia/packages/JuMP/Y4piv/src/Containers/DenseAxisArray.jl:325
 [6] top-level scope
   @ REPL[33]:1
```

After (this PR):
```julia
julia> daa[1, InlineString("b")]
3

Allows e.g. looking up `String` key with another `AbstractString` 
- The use-case here is when a DenseAxisArray is created with `InlineString` keys but where this is an implementation detail and we want to be able to index with a regular `String`
  
Before:
```julia
julia> using JuMP, InlineStrings

julia> daa = JuMP.Containers.DenseAxisArray(reshape(1:6, 2, :), 1:2, ["a","b","c"])
2-dimensional DenseAxisArray{Int64,2,...} with index sets:
    Dimension 1, 1:2
    Dimension 2, ["a", "b", "c"]
And data, a 2×3 Matrix{Int64}:
 1  3  5
 2  4  6

julia> daa[1, "b"]
3

julia> daa[1, InlineString("b")]
ERROR: KeyError: key "b" not found
Stacktrace:
 [1] getindex(#unused#::JuMP.Containers._AxisLookup{Dict{String, Int64}}, key::String1)
   @ JuMP.Containers ~/.julia/packages/JuMP/Y4piv/src/Containers/DenseAxisArray.jl:18
 [2] _getindex_recurse(data::Tuple{JuMP.Containers._AxisLookup{Dict{String, Int64}}}, keys::Tuple{String1}, condition::JuMP.Containers.var"#10#12")
   @ JuMP.Containers ~/.julia/packages/JuMP/Y4piv/src/Containers/DenseAxisArray.jl:309
 [3] _getindex_recurse(data::Tuple{JuMP.Containers._AxisLookup{Tuple{Int64, Int64}}, JuMP.Containers._AxisLookup{Dict{String, Int64}}}, keys::Tuple{Int64, String1}, condition::JuMP.Containers.var"#10#12")
   @ JuMP.Containers ~/.julia/packages/JuMP/Y4piv/src/Containers/DenseAxisArray.jl:308
 [4] to_index(A::JuMP.Containers.DenseAxisArray{Int64, 2, Tuple{UnitRange{Int64}, Vector{String}}, Tuple{JuMP.Containers._AxisLookup{Tuple{Int64, Int64}}, JuMP.Containers._AxisLookup{Dict{String, Int64}}}}, idx::Tuple{Int64, String1})
   @ JuMP.Containers ~/.julia/packages/JuMP/Y4piv/src/Containers/DenseAxisArray.jl:318
 [5] getindex(::JuMP.Containers.DenseAxisArray{Int64, 2, Tuple{UnitRange{Int64}, Vector{String}}, Tuple{JuMP.Containers._AxisLookup{Tuple{Int64, Int64}}, JuMP.Containers._AxisLookup{Dict{String, Int64}}}}, ::Int64, ::String1)
   @ JuMP.Containers ~/.julia/packages/JuMP/Y4piv/src/Containers/DenseAxisArray.jl:325
 [6] top-level scope
   @ REPL[33]:1
```

After (this PR):
```julia
julia> daa[1, InlineString("b")]
3

julia> daa2 = JuMP.Containers.DenseAxisArray(reshape(1:6, 2, :), 1:2, InlineString.(["a","b","c"]));

julia> daa2[1, "b"]
3

> Perhaps it is preferable to add an explicit AbstractString method so we're clear in why we're doing this.

We could do that if you prefer. I didn't do that because it'd be more code, and also because it's conceivable that other types will be purposefully implemented to be hash-equal (like InlineStrings are with Strings). Essentially I presumed that `_AxisLookup` was meant to have the same lookup behaviour as a `Dict`.

3

   @ JuMP.Containers ~/.julia/packages/JuMP/Y4piv/src/Containers/DenseAxisArray.jl:318
 [5] getindex(::JuMP.Containers.DenseAxisArray{Int64, 2, Tuple{UnitRange{Int64}, Vector{String}}, Tuple{JuMP.Containers._AxisLookup{Tuple{Int64, Int64}}, JuMP.Containers._AxisLookup{Dict{String, Int64}}}}, ::Int64, ::String1)

> So this would allow someone to create a set with integers and index with floats?

Yes, i suppose that is true. And likewise allow users to have an index set with e.g. `Int32` integers and able to lookup with `Int64` integers, which does seem pretty reasonable to me. As with the `InlineString` case sometime the actual type used is a (performance-related) implementation detail that we don't want users to have to think about i.e. they just write `"str"` or `123`","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jump-dev/JuMP.jl/issues/comments/1196549239/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

What is this PR?

This PR adds some recommendations to the JuMP style guide that we think you should follow when working with abstract types and method arguments in Julia.

## Why

The  [not-julia](https://yuri.is/not-julia/) article made some very good points.

There was a long discussion on Discourse:
 * https://discourse.julialang.org/t/discussion-on-why-i-no-longer-recommend-julia-by-yuri-vishnevsky/81151
and also one on HackerNews. Here's the relevant JuMP issue: https://github.com/jump-dev/JuMP.jl/issues/2988

## Feedback

We're in no rush to merge this, so please chime in with feedback. This will likely take a few rounds of edits to get right.

Closes case it's of interest, the recently released [SciML style](https://github.com/SciML/SciMLStyle) also has a section on recommendations e.g. https://github.com/SciML/SciMLStyle#errors-should-be-caught-as-high-as-possible-and-error-messages-should-be-contextualized-for-newcommers

I'm not sure what the name is for these kind of guidelines (principles? / philosophies? / dogmas?!), different from the syntactic style suggestions (things JuliaFormatter could in principle automate, "put space around binary operators" etc), but i think it's interesting that these things are being made more explicit. I suppose, like with syntactic style, it may be interesting in time to see where different large projects agree/disagree on various points.

cost of named variables and constraints in Performance Tips","user":{"login":"nickrobinson251","id":13448787,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEzNDQ4Nzg3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/13448787?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251","html_url":"https://github.com/nickrobinson251","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2022-05-06T12:20:30Z","updated_at":"2022-05-06T12:20:30Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"When building a model with millions of variables and constraints, I see model build time roughly half when switching to using anonymous variables and constraints. 

An example model has size
```jl
Variables: 2574376 
Objective function type: AffExpr
`AffExpr`-in-`EqualTo{Float64}`: 1097232 constraints
`AffExpr`-in-`GreaterThan{Float64}`: 315984 constraints
`AffExpr`-in-`LessThan{Float64}`: 1445558 constraints
`VariableRef`-in-`GreaterThan{Float64}`: 1721368 constraints
`VariableRef`-in-`LessThan{Float64}`: 40032 constraints
`VariableRef`-in-`ZeroOne`: 40032 constraints 
```

And an example code change would be:
```diff
-        @variable(model, foo[z in zones, t in times] >= 0)
+        model[:foo] = foo = @variable(model, [z in zones, t in times], lower_bound=0)
```

Changing all `@variable` and `@constraint` calls similarly, I see differences in model build time like below:

|  | time | allocs | mem |
| ------ | ------ | ------ | ------ |
| names | 36.214 s | 131.82 M | 7.19 GiB |
|      no names | 16.652 s | 67.09 M | 4.05 GiB |
| relative |  2.175x | 1.965x | 1.775x |

Depending on the model we're building, the time speed up varies (~1.3 - 2x) but the reduction of allocations is pretty consistently about a ~2x reduction.

The different types of "names" and the behaviour of the macros regarding names is documented
- for variables: https://jump.dev/JuMP.jl/stable/manual/variables/#String-names,-symbolic-names,-and-bindings
- for constraints: https://jump.dev/JuMP.jl/stable/manual/constraints/#String-names,-symbolic-names,-and-bindings

So actually making this change was fairly easy. And this discovery is a very welcome performance improvement!

However, i don't think this significant performance consideration is mentioned anywhere in the docs. And in particular it does't seem to be mentioned in [the Performance Tips](https://jump.dev/JuMP.jl/stable/tutorials/getting_started/performance_tips/)

I think the overhead of named variables/constraints is known (xref https://github.com/jump-dev/JuMP.jl/issues/2817#issuecomment-993085257), so I think worth documenting given it can be a significant build-time cost and is avoidable with relatively small user code changes.

(p.s. i'm opening this just before some time off so may not be quick to respond)","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jump-dev/JuMP.jl/issues/2973/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jump-dev/JuMP.jl/issues/2973/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null}}

> 
> Then you could write `@variable(model, foo[z in zones, t in times] >= 0, base_name = nothing)`

Yeah, i like that idea for a few reasons:
- it'd make it much easier to update code to use / not used names (just set / remove `base_name = ...`)
- relatedly, it'd make it easy for packages to expose the choice to users (for development/debugging purposes) e.g. packages could write variables like `@variable(model, foo[z in zones, t in times] >= 0, base_name = use_names ? "foo" : nothing)`
  - aside: perhaps this use-case motivates `base_name = false` over `base_name = nothing` for disabling names, so that this could just written as `@variable(model, foo[z in zones, t in times] >= 0, base_name = use_names)`, which does seem less repetitive given `foo` is already written.
- This (I'm guessing) would allow a way to disable names, while still creating a binding to a Julia variable

The imagined behaviour i'm talking about in the last point is:
```julia
julia> @variable(model, foo[z in 1:2, t in 1:3], base_name=nothing));

julia> foo
2×3 Matrix{VariableRef}:
 _[1]  _[3]  _[5]
 _[2]  _[4]  _[6]
 
 julia> model[:foo] == foo
true
```

avoiding the `model[:foo] = foo = @variable(...)` part of the change mentioned in the first post.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jump-dev/JuMP.jl/issues/comments/1120217181/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jump-dev/JuMP.jl/pulls/comments/873238145","pull_request_review_id":973280443,"id":873238145,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOAF3Xtc40DI6B","diff_hunk":"@@ -159,3 +159,39 @@ model = Model()
 # If we use the [`@expression`](@ref) macro, we get many fewer allocations:
 
 @allocated @expression(model, x[1] + x[2] + x[3])
+
+# ## Disable string names
+
+# By default, JuMP creates `String` names for variables and constraints and
+# passes these to the solver. The benefit of passing names is that it improves
+# the readability of log messages from the solver (e.g., "variable x has invalid
+# bounds" instead of "variable v1203 has invalid bounds"), but for larger models
+# the overhead of passing names can be non-trivial.
+
+# Disable the passing of names from JuMP to the solver by setting
+# `set_string_name = false` in the [`@variable`](@ref) and [`@constraint`](@ref)
+# macros.
+
+model = Model();
+@variable(model, x, set_string_name = false)
+
+#-
+
+@constraint(model, c, 2x <= 1, set_string_name = false)
+
+# Note that this doesn't change now symbolic names and bindings are stored:","path":"docs/src/tutorials/getting_started/performance_tips.jl","position":24,"original_position":24,"commit_id":"aa390ee22b3c00f1ab8df7542c9b6957979df96f","original_commit_id":"aa390ee22b3c00f1ab8df7542c9b6957979df96f","user":{"login":"nickrobinson251","id":13448787,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEzNDQ4Nzg3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/13448787?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251","html_url":"https://github.com/nickrobinson251","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"typo?

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jump-dev/JuMP.jl/pulls/comments/873238250","pull_request_review_id":973280443,"id":873238250,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOAF3Xtc40DI7q","diff_hunk":"@@ -159,3 +159,39 @@ model = Model()
 # If we use the [`@expression`](@ref) macro, we get many fewer allocations:
 
 @allocated @expression(model, x[1] + x[2] + x[3])
+
+# ## Disable string names
+
+# By default, JuMP creates `String` names for variables and constraints and
+# passes these to the solver. The benefit of passing names is that it improves
+# the readability of log messages from the solver (e.g., "variable x has invalid
+# bounds" instead of "variable v1203 has invalid bounds"), but for larger models
+# the overhead of passing names can be non-trivial.
+
+# Disable the passing of names from JuMP to the solver by setting
+# `set_string_name = false` in the [`@variable`](@ref) and [`@constraint`](@ref)
+# macros.
+
+model = Model();
+@variable(model, x, set_string_name = false)
+
+#-
+
+@constraint(model, c, 2x <= 1, set_string_name = false)
+
+# Note that this doesn't change now symbolic names and bindings are stored:
+
+x
+
+#-
+
+model[:x]
+
+#-
+
+x === model[:x]
+
+# !!! info
+#     For more information on the difference betweene string names, symbolic","path":"docs/src/tutorials/getting_started/performance_tips.jl","position":37,"original_position":37,"commit_id":"aa390ee22b3c00f1ab8df7542c9b6957979df96f","original_commit_id":"aa390ee22b3c00f1ab8df7542c9b6957979df96f","user":{"login":"nickrobinson251","id":13448787,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEzNDQ4Nzg3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/13448787?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251","html_url":"https://github.com/nickrobinson251","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickrobinson251/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"```suggestion

I've updated the model building code mentioned in #2973. I don't see the same performance gains using this new syntax, but it's most of the way there so it's almost certainly human-error on my part (i'm looking into it), but just in case: is there anyway using this keyword  like `@constraint(model, con[...], x[...] <= y[...], set_string_names-=false)` wouldn't save as much work as using `model[:con] = @constraint(model, [...], x[...] <= y[...])`?

One thought does come to mind when updating all that code: 
in that use-case, "set_string_names" is something we actually want to set _at the `Model` level_ (not at a per-`@variable`/`@constraint` level) i.e. what would be optimal for that code is if we could write e.g. `model = Model(; set_string_names=false)` and then, for that `model`, `@variable(model, x)` would be equivalent to `@variable(model, x, set_string_name=false)`, and avoids adding `set_string_name=false` to tens of `@variable`/`@constraint` calls in the code-base (and reduces the chance someone forgets to add `set_string_name=false` when adding a new `@variable`/`@constraint`).

so the macro logic would be something like 
```diff
new_name_code = if isempty(set_string_name_kw_args)
-        name_code   
+        model.set_string_names ? name_code : ""
    else
        Expr(:if, esc(set_string_name_kw_args[1].args[2]), name_code, "")
    end
```
(assuming `model` (somehow) stores a `set_string_names::Bool`)","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jump-dev/JuMP.jl/issues/comments/1127508984/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

```jl

    Building Gurobi → `/builds/invenia/research/Foo.jl.tmp/depot/scratchspaces/44cfe95a-1eb2-52ea-b672-e2afdf69b78f/67d273949798c82781756b54af20d73b4dbe9e3f/build.log`

ERROR: LoadError: SystemError: opening file "/builds/invenia/research/Foo.jl.tmp/depot/packages/Gurobi/PnexG/deps/deps.jl": No such file or directory

#### Use-case 

I'm trying to build and deploy documentation which has `Gurobi.jl` as a dependency. 

We don't expect the Gurobi Solver to actually be installed on the CI, we just want Gurobi.jl loadable (not usable) for documenting some things. Specifically, we have a few Gurobi-related helper functions (e.g. delayed retries on usage limits) that are loaded using Requires.jl if Gurobi.jl is present, and we want to include the docstrings for these in the hosted docs.

#### Use-case 

I'm trying to build and deploy documentation which has `Gurobi.jl` as a dependency. 

We don't expect the Gurobi Solver to actually be installed on the CI, we just want Gurobi.jl loadable (not usable) for documenting some things. Specifically, we have a few Gurobi-related helper functions (e.g. delayed retries on usage limits) that are loaded using Requires.jl if Gurobi.jl is present, and we want to include the docstrings for these in the hosted docs.

We have Gurobi.jl in the `docs/Project.toml`, and set the `GUROBI_JL_SKIP_LIB_CHECK` environment variable on CI.

This setup worked fine with v0.10.3, but with v0.11 we get the error above.
